questions.text: How do you reference an output from a module called 'vpc' in root configuration?

options.text: vpc.output_name
module.vpc.output_name
output.vpc.output_name
modules.vpc.output_name

questions.explanation: Module outputs are referenced with module.module_name.output_name. The module label matches the name used in the module block. Only values declared in the module's outputs.tf are accessible; internal resource attributes are encapsulated.

questions.text: What happens when you add a new module call and run 'terraform init'?

options.text: Terraform applies the module immediately
Terraform downloads and initializes the module source
An error occurs — modules must be added before init
terraform plan automatically runs

questions.explanation: When you add a module block and run terraform init, Terraform downloads the module from its source (registry, Git, local path) and initializes it. This is required before terraform plan or apply can work with the new module.

questions.text: What is the recommended file structure for a reusable Terraform module?

options.text: A single main.tf with all resources, variables, and outputs
main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, and optionally versions.tf
resources.tf, inputs.tf, returns.tf
module.tf containing all module logic

questions.explanation: Standard module structure uses main.tf (resources), variables.tf (input variables), outputs.tf (output values), and versions.tf (provider requirements). README.md documents usage. This convention is enforced by the Terraform Registry and most linting tools.

questions.text: How do you call a module from the Terraform Registry (e.g., terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws)?

options.text: module "eks" { registry = "terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws" }
import "terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws" as eks
module "eks" { source = "terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws", version = "~> 20.0" }
terraform get terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws

questions.explanation: Registry modules use source = "namespace/module/provider" format. Always specify a version constraint to avoid unexpected updates. After adding the module block, run terraform init to download it.

questions.text: What is the double slash (//) used for in a Git module source?

options.text: It's a comment indicator
It separates the repository URL from the subdirectory path
It indicates the branch to use
It specifies the module version

questions.explanation: In Git module sources, // separates the repository URL from the subdirectory: github.com/org/repo//modules/vpc?ref=v1.0. The single slash is part of the URL path; the double slash marks where the subdirectory path begins. The ?ref= query parameter specifies the tag, branch, or commit.

questions.text: Can you use for_each with module blocks?

options.text: No — each module block creates exactly one instance
Yes — for_each on a module block creates multiple instances of the module
Yes — but only with count, not for_each
Only if the module is from the Terraform Registry

questions.explanation: Module blocks support both count and for_each meta-arguments. module.name[key].output_name references instances. This enables creating multiple environments, regions, or tenants from a single module definition.

questions.text: What is module encapsulation in Terraform?

options.text: Encrypting module variables with Terraform Vault
A module's internal resources are only accessible via declared outputs
Preventing module code from being modified
Limiting which providers a module can use

questions.explanation: Module encapsulation means that resources created inside a module are not directly accessible from the calling module — only values declared as outputs are exposed. This creates a clean interface, hides implementation details, and allows module internals to change without breaking callers.

questions.text: What does 'sensitive = true' in an output block do?

options.text: Encrypts the value in the state file
Redacts the value from CLI output and logs
Prevents the output from being used as a module input
Marks the output as read-only

questions.explanation: sensitive = true on an output redacts its value in terraform plan/apply output (shown as '(sensitive value)'). The value is still stored in state. When used as module input, receiving modules don't automatically treat it as sensitive — they must also mark their variable as sensitive.

options.text: Downloads provider plugins
Downloads and updates module sources without reinitializing providers
Retrieves the current state from the backend
Gets the latest version of Terraform

questions.explanation: terraform get downloads and updates module sources into the .terraform/modules directory. It's a subset of terraform init — it handles only modules, not providers or backend initialization. Use it after adding or changing module sources when providers don't need reinitialization.

questions.text: Where should provider requirements (required_providers) be declared in a module?

options.text: In each resource block that uses the provider
In a versions.tf file with a terraform {} block, but not provider {} configuration
Only in the root module
In module/meta.tf

questions.explanation: Modules should declare their required_providers in a versions.tf file to specify minimum provider versions. However, modules should NOT contain provider {} configuration blocks — provider configuration is the caller's responsibility. This keeps modules flexible and reusable across different provider setups.
